name: Pantano del Casar Loop Trail
name_original: Vuelta al Embalse del Casar — circular desde Casar de Cáceres
description: Jog the difficult 10.0-mile Pantano del Casar Loop Trail, gaining 331 feet in 1 hour and 49 minutes around the reservoir.
region_intro: Jogging around Casar De Cáceres offers a diverse landscape characterized by extensive holm oak forests and Mediterranean scrubland, forming the unique "dehesa" ecosystem. The region features striking granite rock formations, particularly around the Los Barruecos Natural Monument, and includes artificial ponds and riverside areas. Runners can also explore sections of the historic Vía de la Plata, an ancient Roman road providing relatively flat paths. This varied terrain provides a range of running experiences from gentle paths to more challenging routes. Best jogging routes around Casar De Cáceres The most popular jogging route is Pantano del Casar Loop Trail, a 10.0 miles (16.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 49 minutes to complete. This difficult route circles the local reservoir, offering expansive views. Another top favourite among local runners is Paseo Alto y la Sierrilla — circular desde el Estadio Príncipe Felipe, a difficult 5.9 miles (9.5 km) path. This circular route provides a varied running experience starting from the Estadio Príncipe Felipe. Local runners also love the Las Charcas Trail, a 2.5 miles (4.0 km) trail leading through areas with small ponds, often completed in about 29 minutes. Jogging around Casar De Cáceres is defined by holm oak forests, granite formations, and the historic Vía de la Plata. The network of routes offers options for different ability levels, from easy runs to more challenging trails. The routes in Casar De Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.7 stars from more than 15 reviews. More than 1400 runners have used komoot to explore Casar De Cáceres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in the Casar de Cáceres area?
A: There are over 120 running routes around Casar de Cáceres, offering a wide variety of options for different preferences and fitness levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running trails around Casar de Cáceres?
A: The terrain around Casar de Cáceres is quite diverse. You'll find extensive &lt;strong&gt;holm oak forests&lt;/strong&gt; (encinares), Mediterranean scrubland, and the unique 'dehesa' ecosystem. Many routes feature striking &lt;strong&gt;granite rock formations&lt;/strong&gt;, especially near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6093807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument&lt;/a&gt;. Sections of the historic &lt;strong&gt;Vía de la Plata&lt;/strong&gt; also provide relatively flat and well-maintained paths.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy run?
A: Yes, Casar de Cáceres offers several easy running routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990937456/las-charcas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Charcas Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a 4.0 km (2.5 miles) easy path that takes about 29 minutes to complete, leading through areas with small ponds.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Casar de Cáceres?
A: The running routes in Casar de Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.7 stars from over 15 reviews. More than 1400 runners have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the natural beauty and diverse landscapes.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in Casar de Cáceres?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345637822/paseo-alto-y-la-sierrilla-circular-desde-el-estadio-principe-felipe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paseo Alto y la Sierrilla — circular desde el Estadio Príncipe Felipe&lt;/a&gt;, a 9.5 km (5.9 miles) route offering a varied running experience.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of water features or lakes?
A: Yes, several routes incorporate water features. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1346409016/pantano-del-casar-loop-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pantano del Casar Loop Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for example, circles the local reservoir, providing expansive views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990937456/las-charcas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Charcas Trail&lt;/a&gt; also passes through areas with small ponds. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31866530" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument loop&lt;/a&gt; features artificial ponds and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4396799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lower Barrueco Pond&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are the running trails in Casar de Cáceres family-friendly?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations vary, many of the easier and moderate routes, particularly those with flatter terrain like sections of the Vía de la Plata or paths around the ponds in Los Barruecos, can be suitable for families. Always check the route's difficulty and length to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running routes in Casar de Cáceres?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many trails in natural areas, especially in the dehesa landscapes and holm oak forests. However, it's always advisable to keep them on a leash, particularly in areas with wildlife or livestock, and to check specific regulations for natural monuments like Los Barruecos to ensure compliance.

Q: What natural landmarks or points of interest can I see along the running routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can explore the unique &lt;strong&gt;granite rock formations&lt;/strong&gt; of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6093807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument&lt;/a&gt;, known for its white stork colonies and as a filming location for *Game of Thrones*. Many routes also traverse beautiful &lt;strong&gt;holm oak forests&lt;/strong&gt; and the traditional 'dehesa' landscape. You might also encounter archaeological sites and anthropomorphic tombs within Los Barruecos.

Q: Are there any routes that start from a specific sports facility?
A: Yes, some routes are conveniently located near sports facilities. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345637822/paseo-alto-y-la-sierrilla-circular-desde-el-estadio-principe-felipe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paseo Alto y la Sierrilla — circular desde el Estadio Príncipe Felipe&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31884597" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Estadio Príncipe Felipe&lt;/a&gt; both start from the Estadio Príncipe Felipe, making them easily accessible for runners.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Casar de Cáceres?
A: The best times for running in Casar de Cáceres are typically spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, so early morning or late evening runs are recommended if visiting during this season. Winters are generally mild, making it possible to run year-round, though some days might be cooler or wetter.

This church is from the 16th century, although the truth is that it was built through a reconstruction and arrangement of another that was already on that same site and that had been built between the 13th and 14th centuries.
The tower is usually frequented by storks with their nests and has a square floor plan.

This swamp offers us an ideal place and paths to run in the middle of nature. The swamp is formed with the waters of the Villaluenga River, and its wetlands are home to different types of birds throughout the different seasons of the year.

Located to the north of the urban area, at the end of Larga Baja street, next to the Vía de la Plata, also known as the Camino de las Barcas, it was a place to stop and shelter pilgrims and passers-by. It would be built between the end of the 15th century and the beginning of the 18th century, but after its significant deterioration, a reform was carried out in the 60s of the 20th century, in which part of the original construction disappeared.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail?
faqs.answer: The Pantano del Casar Loop Trail is approximately 16 kilometers (10 miles) long. For jogging, it typically takes around 1 hour and 50 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and any stops you make.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this jogging trail?
faqs.answer: While specific details for this exact trail are limited, similar trails in the region often feature a mix of surfaces. You might encounter sections of track paths, some asphalt, and potentially mountain paths. Be prepared for varied conditions, which could include overgrown areas, muddy sections, and creek crossings, especially after rain. The scenery can shift from open areas to denser foliage.

faqs.question: Is the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as 'difficult' for jogging. With a distance of 16 kilometers and varied terrain, it might be challenging for absolute beginners. Those new to jogging or long-distance trails should consider their fitness level before attempting the full loop.

faqs.question: What landmarks or points of interest can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: Along the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail, you can discover several points of interest. These include the Church of the Assumption, El Bujío, the scenic Pantano del Casar itself, and the Hermitage of Santiago.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other trails. Notably, 68% of the loop follows the Circunvalación del pantano. It also crosses parts of the Mtb Vía de la Plata, Vía de la Plata hiking - 02 Merida to Salamanca, Cañada Real Soriana Occidental, Cañada Real de Cáceres, Plaza Llanada, and Cañada Real del Casar.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this trail are not detailed, it is a circular route starting and ending near Casar de Cáceres. You would typically look for parking within or on the outskirts of Casar de Cáceres to begin your jog.

faqs.question: Is the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, trails in this region are dog-friendly, but it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through populated areas, near livestock, or in nature reserves. Please ensure you clean up after your pet to maintain the trail for everyone.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to jog the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail would likely be during spring (March to May) or autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are generally milder, making for more comfortable conditions. Summer can be very hot, and winter might bring colder, potentially wetter weather, leading to muddy sections.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this trail?
faqs.answer: There are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access and jog the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail. It is generally open to the public.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants near the trail for after my jog?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the trail starts and ends near Casar de Cáceres, you will find various cafes, pubs, and restaurants within the town where you can refuel and relax after your jog.

faqs.question: What kind of natural features can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The trail offers views of the Pantano del Casar (reservoir) itself. Depending on the season, you might experience changing landscapes from open areas to denser foliage, and potentially observe local flora and fauna around the water and along the paths.
